skill.name: game-development
skill.description: Guides game development planning across web, mobile, PC, VR, AR, and multiplayer projects.
skill.summary: Route game projects to platform-specific guidance and core development principles.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and shell execution patterns, but review found documentation text and markdown formatting rather than executable behavior. The only retained risk factor is external command capability because two skill manifests declare Bash access, with no malicious command guidance found.

low_findings.title: Bash Tool Permission Declared
low_findings.description: The main skill and multiplayer sub-skill declare Bash in allowed-tools. This is not malicious by itself, but it grants external command capability if an agent follows the skill metadata.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Bash permission is directly visible in the skill front matter. I found no instructions to execute dangerous commands or exfiltrate data, so the risk remains low.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Weak Cryptography Static Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts matched ordinary game development terms and markdown prose, such as descriptions, desktop guidance, design references, and spatial hash discussion. No cryptographic API usage or insecure hash implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation text, table entries, or headings. They do not call cryptographic functions or define cryptographic behavior.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Markdown Backtick Execution Matches
low_findings.description: The shell execution alerts matched fenced code blocks, inline skill route names, and ASCII decision trees. These are markdown formatting elements, not executable Ruby or shell backtick calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited backticks appear in markdown documentation and routing examples. No Ruby source file or command substitution context exists in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Dismissed System Reconnaissance Static Matches
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts matched benign platform, performance, and gameplay terms in documentation. No commands collect host information, enumerate files, or inspect the execution environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe game design constraints such as timing, Android ARCore, camera movement, and object pooling. They contain no system discovery behavior.

content.value_statement: Game projects need early choices about platform, engine, input, performance, and networking. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical game development planning.
actual_capabilities: Routes projects to web, mobile, PC, VR, AR, and multiplayer guidance.
Explains game loop structure, fixed timestep logic, and rendering budgets.
Compares common game patterns such as state machines, events, ECS, and object pooling.
Provides platform guidance for browser games, mobile games, desktop games, and immersive games.
Outlines multiplayer architecture, synchronization, lag compensation, and anti-cheat principles.
Highlights performance targets for 60 FPS games and 90 FPS VR experiences.
limitations: It provides planning guidance, not complete game source code.
Several routed sub-skills are referenced but not included in this package.
Engine and browser version guidance may need updates as platforms change.
It does not replace platform certification, store review, or legal compliance checks.

use_cases.title: Choose a Game Platform
use_cases.description: Compare web, mobile, PC, VR, AR, and multiplayer constraints before starting implementation.

use_cases.title: Plan a Multiplayer Prototype
use_cases.description: Select an architecture, synchronization model, update rate, and server authority approach.

use_cases.title: Review Performance Risks
use_cases.description: Check frame budgets, asset loading, input abstraction, thermal limits, and VR comfort constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a Starting Platform
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the game-development skill to help me choose a target platform for this game idea: [describe the game]. Compare fit, risks, and first prototype steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Game Loop
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the game-development skill to outline a fixed timestep game loop for [game type]. Include input, update, rendering, and performance budget guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Multiplayer Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the multiplayer guidance to design networking for [game concept]. Recommend server authority, synchronization strategy, update rates, and anti-cheat checks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Cross-Platform Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the full game-development skill to review this technical plan: [paste plan]. Identify platform risks, missing systems, performance issues, and better routing choices.

output_examples.input: I want to build a browser-based 2D platformer.
output: Start with web game guidance because the browser is the deployment target.
Use a simple fixed timestep loop, input actions, object pooling, and progressive asset loading.
Plan WebGL fallback if advanced rendering is required.

output_examples.input: I want a competitive multiplayer shooter prototype.
output: Use an authoritative dedicated server model for competitive play.
Synchronize player input and critical state, then add prediction and reconciliation.
Validate movement, shots, inventory changes, and timing on the server.

output_examples.input: I need to evaluate a VR game concept.
output: Prioritize comfort, stable frame rate, and player-controlled camera movement.
Target 90 FPS where possible and design locomotion with teleport or snap turning options.
Test world scale, reach distance, UI readability, and interaction precision early.

best_practices: Start with the platform sub-skill that matches the deployment target.
Define player actions before mapping keyboard, controller, touch, or VR inputs.
Profile before optimizing and keep frame budgets visible during implementation.
anti_patterns: Do not choose an engine only because it is popular.
Do not trust multiplayer clients with authoritative game state.
Do not load all assets upfront when progressive loading is viable.

faq.question: Does this skill generate complete games?
faq.answer: No. It provides planning, routing, architecture, and review guidance for game development tasks.

faq.question: Which platforms does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers web, mobile, PC, console-oriented desktop guidance, VR, AR, and multiplayer topics.

faq.question: Can it help choose an engine?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares engines such as Unity, Godot, Unreal, Phaser, PixiJS, Three.js, and Babylon.js.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. The multiplayer section emphasizes server authority, anti-cheat checks, and not trusting clients.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it for implementation?
faq.answer: Yes. They can use it to structure plans and reviews, then apply the guidance to project files.

faq.question: What should users verify separately?
faq.answer: Users should verify current engine versions, store rules, browser support, and platform certification requirements.
